scenario_name: Scenario 11 — Queue Backpressure and Consumer Lag
justification: All pass criteria met: throughput gap clearly identified (300/min inbound vs ~20/min processing, 15x deficit), unbounded queue growth consequence described (~280 msgs/min accumulating), visibility timeout double-processing risk identified with math (10×4s=40s > 30s timeout), missing DLQ identified as a blocker, queue depth metric gap identified, autoscaling recommended (minimum 15 worker instances), verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Throughput math is precise and well-articulated — 15x deficit framing makes the severity unmistakable
Visibility timeout analysis includes concrete worst-case batch timing calculation
DLQ absence framed as a blocker with specific recommendation (maxReceiveCount=3)
Queue depth metric gap explicitly called out with operational consequence — failure mode would be invisible
Goes beyond rubric requirements with idempotency concerns, circuit breaker gaps, graceful degradation, and OOM risks
Escalations are well-routed — infrastructure gaps to MJ, observability gaps to Shaq
On-call debugging narrative effectively illustrates why current observability is insufficient
Confidence section honestly flags assumptions and low-confidence areas rather than overclaiming

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: throughput gap clearly identified (300/min vs 15-20/min with 280/min deficit calculated), unbounded queue growth described (16,800 messages/hour), visibility timeout double-processing risk identified with math (10×2-4s=20-40s vs 30s timeout), missing DLQ identified as a blocker (CRITICAL label, escalated to Coach K), queue depth metric gap explicitly called out as CRITICAL, autoscaling recommended (15-20 instances or autoscaling policy), verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Throughput math is precise and well-articulated — includes per-hour accumulation rate (16,800 messages/hour)
Visibility timeout analysis is exceptionally thorough — shows the batch math proving duplicates are near-certain, not just possible
DLQ absence correctly framed as compounding the throughput deficit, not just a standalone issue
Queue depth metric gap explicitly tied to operational blindness — explains that the primary failure mode is unmonitorable
Goes well beyond rubric requirements with additional failure modes: OOM on large images, circuit breaker absence, downstream dependency failures, crash mid-batch
Escalations are well-structured with clear routing and actionable next steps
Confidence section honestly acknowledges uncertainty about parallelism and peak load duration

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Throughput gap clearly identified as 300/min inbound vs 15-20/min processing with exact deficit calculated at ~280/min. (2) Queue growth consequence described with specific projection of ~16,800 backlogged messages within 1 hour. (3) Visibility timeout double-processing risk identified with mathematical proof (10×4s=40s > 30s timeout). (4) Missing DLQ identified as a blocker with poison message consequences explained. (5) Queue depth metric gap explicitly called out as CRITICAL. (6) Autoscaling recommended with specific capacity target of 15-20 concurrent workers. (7) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Throughput math is exceptionally precise — calculates exact deficit rate and projects 1-hour backlog size
Visibility timeout risk framed as 'mathematical certainty, not a theoretical risk' — exactly the right framing
DLQ absence correctly escalated as infrastructure gap with compounding effect on throughput deficit
Goes beyond rubric requirements with idempotency concerns, graceful shutdown handling, and downstream failure analysis
Escalations are well-routed to appropriate team members (MJ for architecture, Shaq for implementation)
Observability review is thorough — identifies not just missing queue depth but also message age, duplicate detection, and processing duration metrics
Provides concrete remediation options for visibility timeout (increase timeout, heartbeating, or reduce batch size) with recommendation
